topics.title: Project and Layout of Sludge Treatment Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCVT504 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING II
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project and Layout of Sludge Treatment Plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sludge Treatment Plant (STP – Sludge Line)** refers to the engineered sequence of physical, chemical, and biological unit processes designed to **reduce volume, stabilize organic matter, and condition the residual solids** (sludge) generated during municipal wastewater treatment, prior to their safe disposal or beneficial reuse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *"Sludge treatment comprises thickening, stabilization, conditioning, dewatering, and disposal — each stage reducing mass, volume, pathogens, and putrescibility, while producing a final biosolids/residue suitable for landfill, incineration, or land application."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Waste Recycler"

Imagine a household kitchen. After cooking, you have:
- **Vegetable peels (Primary Sludge)** – settle quickly at the bottom of the sink.
- **Greasy water with food particles (Secondary/Activated Sludge)** – floats and is hard to handle.
- **Leftover bones and fibrous waste (Tertiary/Chemical Sludge)** – needs grinding.

You don't throw this directly into the dustbin (it stinks, attracts flies, leaks). Instead, you:
1. **Drain off water** (Thickening) – so it occupies less space.
2. **Compost it for weeks** (Stabilization/Digestion) – so it stops smelling.
3. **Add a drying agent like sawdust** (Conditioning) – to make it easier to handle.
4. **Press it in a cloth** (Dewatering) – to squeeze out the last drops of water.
5. **Dispose/dry the cake** (Final Disposal) – into the garden or bin.

A **Sludge Treatment Plant** is the **municipal-scale, industrial version** of this kitchen recycler. The "drain" is a **Gravity Thickener**, the "compost bin" is an **Anaerobic Digester**, the "sawdust" is a **Polymer (conditioner)**, and the "cloth press" is a **Filter Press / Centrifuge**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Design

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Specific gravity of sludge solids | **1.01 – 1.05** (primary), **1.005 – 1.02** (activated) | – |
| Dry solids content – raw sludge | **2 – 5 %** | % |
| Dry solids content – thickened sludge | **5 – 10 %** | % |
| Dry solids content – digested sludge | **3 – 6 %** | % |
| Dry solids content – dewatered cake | **20 – 35 %** | % |
| Dry solids content – dried sludge | **> 90 %** | % |
| Volatile solids reduction in anaerobic digester | **50 – 60 %** | % |
| SRT for mesophilic anaerobic digester | **15 – 30 days** | days |
| Mesophilic digestion temperature | **35 ± 2 °C** | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** A common 14-mark question asks to design a thickener or digester. Always quote these typical ranges as the basis for **assumptions** before any design calculation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources and Classification of Sludge

The sludge generated at various stages of a wastewater treatment plant differs sharply in characteristics:

| Sludge Type | Source | Solids Conc. (%) | VS/TS Ratio | Dewaterability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Sludge | Primary Clarifier (settling) | 2 – 6 | 0.60 – 0.80 | Good |
| Secondary (Activated) Sludge | Aeration tank / Secondary clarifier | 0.5 – 1.5 | 0.70 – 0.90 | Poor |
| Trickling Filter Humus | TF Secondary clarifier | 1 – 4 | 0.60 – 0.75 | Moderate |
| Chemical Sludge | Phosphorus removal coagulation | 0.5 – 3 | 0.20 – 0.40 | Variable |
| Digested Sludge | Outlet of Anaerobic Digester | 3 – 6 | 0.40 – 0.55 | Moderate |

### 2.2 The Five Sequential Unit Operations

The treatment train follows a strict **Volume → Stability → Dewaterability** logic:

**Step 1 — Sludge Thickening**
- **Purpose:** Increase solids concentration from ~1–2 % to **4–8 %** to reduce downstream tankage.
- **Methods:** Gravity Thickener (GT), Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF), Gravity Belt Thickener (GBT), Centrifugal Thickener.
- **Design loading for GT:** **25 – 35 kg/m²·day** (typical KTU value).
- **Solid capture efficiency:** ≥ **90 %**.

**Step 2 — Sludge Stabilization**
- **Purpose:** Destroy pathogens, eliminate putrescibility, reduce odour, and reduce volatile solids (VS).
- **Methods:**
  - *Anaerobic Digestion (preferred for large plants)*
  - *Aerobic Digestion (for small plants)*
  - *Lime Stabilization (chemical)*
  - *Composting*
- **Anaerobic digestion equation (simplified):**

$$\text{Organic Matter} \xrightarrow{\text{Anaerobic bacteria}} \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{New biomass}$$

**Step 3 — Sludge Conditioning**
- **Purpose:** Prepare sludge for dewatering by flocculating fine particles.
- **Methods:** Chemical (polymers like polyacrylamide), thermal (heat treatment at 175–230 °C, 1–4 MPa), or freeze–thaw conditioning.

**Step 4 — Sludge Dewatering**
- **Purpose:** Reduce moisture from ~95 % to **65 – 80 %** (cake solids 20 – 35 %).
- **Methods:** Sludge Drying Beds, Centrifuge, Belt Filter Press, Plate & Frame Filter Press, Vacuum Filter.

**Step 5 — Sludge Disposal / Reuse**
- Landfilling, incineration, land application (biosolids), construction material (e.g., bricks, cement).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU ESE under Module 4.

**Notation Convention:**
- $Q$ = sewage flow, $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- $X$ = solids concentration, $\text{mg/L}$ or $\text{kg/m}^3$
- $M_s$ = mass of dry solids, $\text{kg/day}$
- $V$ = volume of sludge, $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- $S$ = specific gravity of sludge
- $p$ = moisture content as fraction
- $G_s$ = specific gravity of solids ≈ **1.02**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_s = Q \times X \times 10^{-3}$ | Dry solids mass (kg/day) from $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{d}$ and $X$ in $\text{mg/L}$ |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{M_s}{\rho_w \cdot S \cdot (1-p)}$ | Volume of wet sludge per day |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{G_s \cdot p + (1-p)}{p + (1-p) \cdot G_s / G_w \cdot \rho_w}$ | Specific gravity of sludge mixture |
| 4 | $V_{thickener} = \dfrac{M_s}{L \times C}$ | Thickener area × depth; $L$ = solids loading (kg/m²·d) |
| 5 | $V_{digester} = M_s \times \theta_c$ | Digester volume = mass × HRT/SRT (days) |
| 6 | $V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times (V_s \text{ added}) - \text{optional offset}$ | Methane yield ≈ 0.35 m³/kg VS destroyed |
| 7 | $V_{digested\,sludge} = V_0 \times \left(1 - 0.5 \cdot \frac{VS_0 - VS_f}{VS_0}\right)$ | Volume reduction post-digestion |
| 8 | $\eta_{VS} = \dfrac{VS_{in} - VS_{out}}{VS_{in}} \times 100$ | Volatile solids reduction efficiency (%) |
| 9 | $A_{drying\,bed} = \dfrac{M_s}{L_{bed}}$ | Area of sludge drying bed |
| 10 | $W_{polymer} = M_s \times \text{dose}$ | Polymer dose typically 2 – 10 kg polymer per tonne DS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used to size thickeners, digesters, drying beds, dewatering units, and to estimate biogas/energy recovery from anaerobic digesters — a hot topic in modern sustainable design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mass of Dry Solids and Volume of Sludge

**Given:** Sewage flow $Q = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$, total suspended solids concentration entering primary tank $X = 250 \text{ mg/L}$, with 60 % removed as primary sludge. Specific gravity of sludge solids $G_s = 1.02$, moisture content $p = 0.95$.

**Step 1 — Compute the mass of dry solids in primary sludge (kg/day).**

$$
M_s = Q \times X \times 10^{-3} \times \text{removal fraction}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
M_s = 10{,}000 \times 250 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.60
$$

$$
M_s = 10{,}000 \times 0.250 \times 0.60 = 1{,}500 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the specific gravity of the sludge mixture.**

Using the standard sludge specific gravity formula:

$$
S = \frac{G_s \cdot p + (1-p)}{p + (1-p) \cdot G_s}
$$

Substituting $G_s = 1.02$, $p = 0.95$:

$$
S = \frac{(1.02)(0.95) + (0.05)}{0.95 + (0.05)(1.02)}
$$

$$
S = \frac{0.969 + 0.05}{0.95 + 0.051} = \frac{1.019}{1.001} = 1.018
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the volume of wet sludge (m³/day).**

$$
V = \frac{M_s}{\rho_w \cdot S \cdot (1-p)}
$$

where $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $(1-p) = 0.05$ (fraction of solids).

$$
V = \frac{1{,}500}{1000 \times 1.018 \times 0.05} = \frac{1{,}500}{50.9} = 29.47 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Always express $(1-p)$ as the **dry solids fraction** (not moisture fraction). Students commonly invert this and lose 1–2 marks per question.

### 3.2 Derivation: Volume Reduction After Anaerobic Digestion

**Given:** Volatile solids (VS) in feed sludge = 70 % of TS, VS destroyed by digestion = **50 %** (typical mesophilic digester). Initial wet sludge volume $V_0 = 29.47 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ from previous derivation.

**Step 1 — Compute the volatile solids reduction.**

$$
\eta_{VS} = \frac{VS_{in} - VS_{out}}{VS_{in}} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the volume reduction model (volatile solids approach).**

$$
V_{out} = V_0 \times \left[ (1 - f_{VS,in}) + f_{VS,in}(1 - \eta_{VS}) \right]
$$

where $f_{VS,in} = 0.70$ (fraction of VS in feed) and $\eta_{VS} = 0.50$.

$$
V_{out} = 29.47 \times \left[ (1 - 0.70) + (0.70)(1 - 0.50) \right]
$$

$$
V_{out} = 29.47 \times \left[ 0.30 + 0.35 \right] = 29.47 \times 0.65
$$

$$
V_{out} = 19.16 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage volume reduction.**

$$
\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{29.47 - 19.16}{29.47} \times 100 = 34.99 \approx 35\%
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Sizing of an Anaerobic Digester

**Given:** Sludge mass $M_s = 1{,}500 \text{ kg DS/day}$, design SRT/HRT $\theta_c = 20$ days, digester temperature $T = 35\,°\text{C}$ (mesophilic).

**Step 1 — Compute digester volume.**

$$
V_{digester} = M_s \times \theta_c
$$

$$
V_{digester} = 1{,}500 \times 20 = 30{,}000 \text{ kg (or m}^3 \text{ equivalent for water-like density)}
$$

Assuming density of sludge $\approx 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$
V_{digester} \approx 30{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (this is too large — students should know digestion is done on VOLUME basis)}
$$

**Step 1 (Corrected) — Volumetric basis:**

Wet sludge feed volume $V_0 = 29.47 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$.

$$
V_{digester} = V_0 \times \theta_c = 29.47 \times 20 = 589.4 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Methane yield estimation (energy recovery).**

Volatile solids destroyed per day:

$$
M_{VS,destroyed} = M_s \times f_{VS} \times \eta_{VS} = 1{,}500 \times 0.70 \times 0.50 = 525 \text{ kg VS/day}
$$

Methane volume generated (using standard yield $0.5 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg VS destroyed}$ at $35\,°\text{C}$):

$$
V_{CH_4} = 525 \times 0.5 = 262.5 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{day}
$$

Energy value (CH₄ calorific value $\approx 35.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3$):

$$
E = 262.5 \times 35.8 = 9{,}397.5 \text{ MJ/day} \approx 2{,}611 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

### 3.4 Sludge Drying Bed Sizing

**Given:** $M_s = 1{,}500 \text{ kg DS/day}$, design solids loading on drying bed $L_{bed} = 120 \text{ kg/m}^2 \cdot \text{year}$ (KTU standard for unconditioned sludge in tropical climate).

**Step 1 — Compute total area.**

$$
A_{bed} = \frac{M_s \times 365}{L_{bed}} = \frac{1{,}500 \times 365}{120}
$$

$$
A_{bed} = \frac{547{,}500}{120} = 4{,}562.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Number of beds (assume each bed = 100 m²).**

$$
n = \frac{4{,}562.5}{100} \approx 46 \text{ beds}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** For smaller plants (< 5 MLD), sludge drying beds are still the most economical option. For larger plants, mechanical dewatering (centrifuge / belt press) is preferred due to land constraints.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Sludge Treatment Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Sludge from Primary Clarifier] --> B[Sludge Thickening\nGravity Thickener or DAF]
    C[WAS from Secondary Clarifier] --> B
    B --> D[Thickened Sludge\n6 to 8 percent DS]
    D --> E[Sludge Stabilization\nAnaerobic Digester]
    E --> F[Digested Sludge\n3 to 5 percent DS]
    F --> G[Sludge Conditioning\nPolymer Addition]
    G --> H[Sludge Dewatering\nBelt Press or Centrifuge]
    H --> I[Dewatered Cake\n20 to 35 percent DS]
    I --> J[Final Disposal\nLandfill or Land Application]
    E --> K[Biogas Collection\nCH4 plus CO2]
    K --> L[Energy Recovery\nBoiler or CHP Unit]
    H --> M[Filtrate Recycle\nBack to Head of Works]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Anaerobic Digester Cross-Section & Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PlantLayout[Sludge Treatment Plant Layout]
        direction TB
        P1[Thickener 1\nPrimary] --> P2[Digester Feed Well]
        P3[Thickener 2\nSecondary WAS] --> P2
        P2 --> P4[Primary Digester\nMesophilic 35 C]
        P4 --> P5[Secondary Digester\nSludge Storage]
        P4 --> P6[Gas Holder\nBiogas Storage]
        P6 --> P7[Gas Utilization\nBoiler and CHP]
        P5 --> P8[Sludge Drying Beds\nor Dewatering Building]
        P8 --> P9[Final Cake Storage\nor Landfill]
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Decision Matrix for Sludge Dewatering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sludge Treatment Plant\nVolume Reduction Required] --> B{Plant Capacity}
    B -->|Less than 5 MLD| C[Drying Beds\nEconomical]
    B -->|5 to 25 MLD| D{Continuous\nOperation?}
    B -->|Greater than 25 MLD| E[Centrifuge or\nBelt Filter Press]
    D -->|Yes| E
    D -->|No| F[Plate Filter Press\nBatch Type]
    C --> G[Cake to Compost\nor Land Application]
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

### 4.4 Schematic Layout (Block-Level) of a Typical STP Sludge Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UnitA[Receiving and Thickening]
        U1[Sludge Sump] --> U2[Gravity Thickener]
    end
    subgraph UnitB[Stabilization]
        U2 --> U3[Anaerobic Digester]
        U3 --> U4[Digested Sludge Sump]
    end
    subgraph UnitC[Conditioning and Dewatering]
        U4 --> U5[Polymer Dosing Unit]
        U5 --> U6[Filter Press or Centrifuge]
    end
    subgraph UnitD[Disposal]
        U6 --> U7[Cake Storage]
        U7 --> U8[Transport to Landfill\nor Incinerator]
    end
    U3 -.biogas.-> U9[Gas Holder]
    U9 -.electricity.-> U10[CHP and Boiler House]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Layout Principles to remember:**
> 1. **Gravity flow** is preferred for sludge wherever possible to reduce pumping costs.
> 2. Thickener should be located **upstream of the digester**.
> 3. Dewatering building should be **closest to disposal point** to minimize cake transport.
> 4. Gas holders must be **upwind** of any ignition source and away from public roads.
> 5. Maintain **2 % slope** for sludge piping to prevent clogging.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three objectives of sludge treatment in a municipal wastewater treatment plant.*

**Model Answer:**

The three primary objectives of sludge treatment are:

1. **Volume Reduction:** Sludge is over 95 % water by weight; thickening and dewatering reduce volume by 60–90 %, lowering transport and disposal costs.
2. **Stabilization:** Biological or chemical processes destroy pathogens, eliminate putrescibility, and suppress odour generation, making sludge safe for handling.
3. **Resource Recovery / Safe Disposal:** Stabilized sludge may be reused as a soil conditioner (biosolids) or for energy recovery via biogas, otherwise safely disposed to landfill without environmental harm.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Do NOT list "treatment" as a generic objective. Examiners expect specific engineering purposes — volume, stability, pathogens, resource recovery.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the role of a sludge thickener? Differentiate between gravity thickener and dissolved air flotation (DAF) thickener.*

**Model Answer:**

A **sludge thickener** concentrates the solids content of sludge (typically from 0.5–2 % to 5–8 % DS) to reduce the volume entering subsequent digestion or dewatering units.

| Feature | Gravity Thickener | Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF) |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Solids settle under gravity | Air bubbles float solids to surface |
| Best suited for | Primary sludge | Activated / waste activated sludge (WAS) |
| Solids loading | 25 – 35 kg/m²·d | 10 – 25 kg/m²·d |
| Typical concentration achieved | 5 – 8 % | 4 – 6 % |
| Footprint | Large | Compact |
| Energy | Low (scraper mechanism) | Higher (recirculation pump + air saturation) |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** A municipal wastewater treatment plant treats $Q = 20 \text{ ML/d}$ of sewage with an average TSS of $300 \text{ mg/L}$. The primary clarifier removes 60 % of TSS. The specific gravity of sludge solids is 1.02, and the moisture content of the underflow sludge is 95 %.
Compute:
(i) Mass of dry solids produced per day. **(3 marks)**
(ii) Specific gravity of the sludge mixture. **(4 marks)**
(iii) Volume of wet sludge per day. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Describe with a neat sketch the working of a **Gravity Sludge Thickener**. List any two design parameters. **(4 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**(i) Mass of dry solids per day**

$$
M_s = Q \times X \times 10^{-3} \times \eta
$$

$$
M_s = 20{,}000 \times 300 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.60
$$

$$
M_s = 20{,}000 \times 0.300 \times 0.60 = 3{,}600 \text{ kg DS/day}
$$

**(ii) Specific gravity of sludge mixture**

With $G_s = 1.02$, $p = 0.95$:

$$
S = \frac{G_s \cdot p + (1-p)}{p + (1-p) \cdot G_s}
$$

$$
S = \frac{(1.02)(0.95) + 0.05}{0.95 + (0.05)(1.02)} = \frac{0.969 + 0.05}{0.95 + 0.051}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1.019}{1.001} = 1.018
$$

**(iii) Volume of wet sludge per day**

$$
V = \frac{M_s}{\rho_w \cdot S \cdot (1-p)} = \frac{3{,}600}{1000 \times 1.018 \times 0.05}
$$

$$
V = \frac{3{,}600}{50.9} = 70.73 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

A **Gravity Sludge Thickener** is a circular tank (similar to a clarifier) equipped with a slowly rotating central drive mechanism fitted with **scraper arms and pickets**.

**Working Principle:**
- Sludge enters through a central feed well.
- Solids settle under gravity; the clarified supernatant overflows into a peripheral launder.
- The slowly rotating scraper arm moves the settled sludge toward a central hopper, from where it is withdrawn as thickened sludge.
- Picket fences (vertical rods) attached to the scraper gently stir the sludge to release trapped water and gas, enhancing compaction.

**Design Parameters:**
1. **Solids loading rate:** 25 – 35 kg/m²·day (for primary sludge).
2. **Hydraulic retention time (HRT):** 24 – 48 hours.
3. **Sludge withdrawal solids concentration:** 5 – 8 % DS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Marks are awarded specifically for the **sketch** (1 mark), **working principle** (2 marks), and **design parameters** (1 mark). Skipping the sketch costs you 1 mark even if the rest is correct.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Anaerobic Digestion** process for sludge stabilization. State the biochemical reactions, design parameters, and factors affecting digester performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A primary digester receives $V_0 = 70.73 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ of sludge (from Question A). The volatile solids fraction is 0.70, and the digester achieves 50 % VS reduction. Design the digester volume for an SRT of 20 days. Estimate the daily methane production at standard conditions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Anaerobic Digestion** is a biological process in which organic matter is decomposed by anaerobic microorganisms in the **absence of oxygen**, producing biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) and a stabilized biosolids product.

**Three stages of biochemical reaction:**

1. **Hydrolysis:** Complex organic polymers (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) are broken down into soluble monomers.
2. **Acidogenesis (Fermentation):** Monomers are converted to volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂ by acidogenic bacteria.
3. **Methanogenesis:** VFAs and H₂/CO₂ are converted to **CH₄** and **CO₂** by methanogenic archaea.

**Net simplified reaction:**

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b\text{N}_c \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{new cells}
$$

**Design Parameters:**
- **Temperature:** Mesophilic 30 – 38 °C (optimum 35 °C) OR Thermophilic 50 – 60 °C.
- **SRT/HRT:** 15 – 30 days (mesophilic); 10 – 20 days (thermophilic).
- **VS loading:** 0.6 – 1.6 kg VS/m³·day.
- **pH:** 6.8 – 7.4.
- **C:N:P ratio:** 100:5:1 (ideal).
- **Mixing:** Continuous mechanical or gas recirculation.

**Factors affecting performance:**
- Temperature fluctuations (must be stable within ±2 °C).
- Loading rate (organic overload → sour digester, pH drop).
- Toxic substances (heavy metals, ammonia, sulfides).
- Mixing intensity (poor mixing → scum formation).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute mass of volatile solids fed per day.**

$$
M_{VS,in} = M_s \times f_{VS} = 3{,}600 \times 0.70 = 2{,}520 \text{ kg VS/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute mass of VS destroyed.**

$$
M_{VS,destroyed} = M_{VS,in} \times \eta_{VS} = 2{,}520 \times 0.50 = 1{,}260 \text{ kg VS/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute digester volume.**

Using volumetric HRT (since $V_0$ is in m³/day):

$$
V_{digester} = V_0 \times \text{HRT} = 70.73 \times 20 = 1{,}414.6 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Compute organic (VS) loading rate check.**

$$
\text{OLR} = \frac{M_{VS,in}}{V_{digester}} = \frac{2{,}520}{1{,}414.6} = 1.78 \text{ kg VS/m}^3 \cdot \text{day}
$$

This is **slightly above** the recommended 1.6 kg VS/m³·d — students may note this and recommend either an increased digester volume (≈ 1575 m³) or the addition of water/recirculation.

**Step 5 — Methane production.**

Using standard yield $Y_{CH_4} = 0.5 \text{ m}^3 \text{CH}_4 / \text{kg VS destroyed}$ at 35 °C:

$$
V_{CH_4} = M_{VS,destroyed} \times Y_{CH_4} = 1{,}260 \times 0.5 = 630 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{day}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):** Students commonly confuse the units — they use $M_s$ (total solids) instead of $M_{VS}$ (volatile solids) for methane yield. Methane comes from VS destruction, **not** total solids. This single error costs 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Sludge volume = $M_s / [\rho_w \cdot S \cdot (1-p)]$** — this is the most-tested formula in Module 4.
- [x] **Sludge thickening** increases solids from 1–2 % to 5–8 %.
- [x] **Anaerobic digestion** reduces VS by 50–60 %; SRT = 15–30 days at 35 °C (mesophilic).
- [x] **Methane yield** ≈ **0.5 m³ CH₄ per kg VS destroyed** (at 35 °C).
- [x] **Sludge dewatering** reaches cake solids of **20–35 %**; drying beds require **120 kg/m²·yr** loading.
- [x] **Polymer dose** for conditioning: **2 – 10 kg polymer per tonne DS**.
- [x] **Specific gravity of sludge** uses the standard mixture formula (not just weighted average).
- [x] **Order of sludge line:** Thickener → Digester → Conditioner → Dewatering → Disposal.
- [x] **Gravity flow** is preferred for sludge; minimum **2 % pipe slope**.
- [x] **Digester gas = 60–70 % CH₄ + 30–40 % CO₂** with trace H₂S; must be **desulfurized** before use.
- [x] **Layout rules:** Thickener upstream of digester; dewatering building near disposal point; gas holder upwind and away from ignition sources.
- [x] **Decant / filtrate / supernatant** from every sludge unit **must be recycled** to the head of the works for treatment.
